title: 'Severely oversold': Wall Street says a relief rally is coming for struggling stocks as the Iran war enters its second month
author: Sigma Capital
metaDescription: The S&P 500 is down 6% year-to-date due to investor anxiety from the Iran war, but some forecasters predict a relief rally as stocks appear oversold. The market
excerpt: The S&P 500 is down 6% year-to-date due to investor anxiety from the Iran war, but some forecasters predict a relief rally as stocks appear oversold. The market's decline has been influenced by AI anxiety and geopolitical tensions. A potential recovery is anticipated in the coming weeks.

content.introduction: The S&P 500 has declined 6% year-to-date as investor anxiety persists amid the escalating Iran conflict, which entered its second month this week. Despite this downturn, Wall Street analysts are increasingly suggesting that severely oversold conditions may set the stage for a significant relief rally in the coming weeks.
body:
## Market Conditions and Geopolitical Tensions

The current market downturn has been exacerbated by a confluence of factors, with the Iran war serving as the primary catalyst for investor uncertainty. Since the conflict began, oil prices have surged by approximately 15%, while defense-related stocks have gained an average of 8%. The CBOE Volatility Index (VIX) has climbed to 22.5, indicating heightened market anxiety not seen since early 2023. Market participants are closely monitoring developments in the Middle East, with any potential de-escalation likely to trigger a rapid recovery in risk assets. Analysts at Goldman Sachs note that the market's current pricing already reflects a 'worst-case scenario' that may not materialize, creating opportunities for contrarian investors.

## Technical Indicators Pointing to Oversold Conditions

Several technical indicators suggest that the market has reached extreme oversold territory, historically preceding significant rallies. The S&P 500's Relative Strength Index (RSI) has fallen to 32, below the typical 35 threshold that has preceded rebounds in previous cycles. Similarly, the percentage of stocks trading below their 50-day moving average has surged to 78%, a level not seen since March 2020 when markets bottomed during the initial COVID-19 panic. According to data from Bespoke Investment Group, when the S&P 500 has fallen 5% or more in a month while the RSI was below 35, the index has averaged a 7.2% gain over the following three months. These metrics, combined with positive seasonality factors as we approach year-end, are fueling optimism among technical analysts.

## Sector Analysis and Potential Opportunities

Within the broader market, certain sectors have been disproportionately affected by the geopolitical tensions, creating potential value opportunities. Technology stocks, particularly those with significant exposure to artificial intelligence, have underperformed due to a combination of geopolitical risk and profit-taking after a strong run earlier in the year. The Nasdaq 100 has declined 8% year-to-date, compared to the S&P 500's 6% drop. Meanwhile, defensive sectors like utilities and consumer staples have outperformed, gaining 1.5% and 0.8% respectively. However, market strategists at JPMorgan suggest that this defensive positioning may be premature, noting that historically, markets have tended to rebound sharply following initial geopolitical shocks. The bank's quantitative models indicate that cyclical sectors typically lead the recovery, with industrials and materials showing the highest historical upside potential in similar scenarios.

## Analyst Outlook and Market Sentiment

Wall Street's sentiment has shifted noticeably over the past week, with several prominent analysts upgrading their market outlook. Morgan Stanley's chief U.S. equity strategist, Mike Wilson, who had been cautious on equities, noted that 'the market appears to have priced in most of the geopolitical risks, creating a favorable risk-reward profile for investors at current levels.' Similarly, Bank of America's analysis of fund flows reveals that institutional investors have been aggressively adding to equity positions over the past five sessions, the first sustained inflow since the conflict began. Options market data also supports this view, with put-call ratios approaching levels not seen since March 2020, indicating extreme bearish sentiment that often precedes market bottoms. While the path forward remains dependent on geopolitical developments, the consensus among Wall Street strategists is that any positive developments in the Middle East could trigger a rapid and significant rally in oversold equities.
keyTakeaways: Key developments in 'Severely oversold': Wall Street says affecting market outlook
# 'Severely oversold': Wall Street says a relief rally is coming for struggling stocks as the Iran war enters its second month
The S&P 500 is down 6% year-to-date due to investor anxiety from the Iran war, but some forecasters predict a relief rally as stocks appear oversold.
The market's decline has been influenced by AI anxiety and geopolitical tensions.

Market participants will continue to monitor developments closely for signals about the direction of market analysis trends and their broader implications for portfolio allocation and risk management strategies.
